summary: My dad was an actor and appeared in many of the Sessue Hayakawa pictures. Sessue Hayakawa was a well-known Japanese actor in Hollywood during the silent film era. I even appeared as a boy in one of his films, which was shot on location at Big Bear Lake. When Sessue Hayakawa's pictures played in theaters, we kids would perform on stage before the show started.

I remember going to Big Bear Lake for a film location when I appeared as a boy in one of the Sessue Hayakawa silent pictures. It was quite an experience being part of those early Hollywood productions. The trip to Big Bear Lake was memorable because it was part of the movie filming. It was during the 1920s when my dad was involved in acting.

I recall the 1960s and the era of John F. Kennedy. During that time, we had bumper stickers on our car that said 'Make Love Not War,' reflecting the sentiments around the Vietnam War. JFK's presidency was a significant backdrop to those years, influencing the culture and politics we experienced. It was a time of change and activism.

During the Vietnam War in the 1960s, I remember we used to put bumper stickers on our car that said 'Make Love Not War.' It was a time of protest and strong feelings against the conflict. The war influenced many aspects of life, including how young people expressed themselves. It was part of the cultural backdrop during the era of John F. Kennedy and beyond.

I had a Barracuda car in the 1960s, and my little brother Dave also had one. The Barracuda was quite old and had many issues like a broken gas tank and speedometer, but he loved it dearly. I remember going to the airport in a Barracuda with Andy, who was a fraternity brother. My dad used to drive a Barracuda too, and it was quite popular among his fraternity friends.

I remember driving up in Fumi's little Datsun with a Siamese cat that was very noisy during the 400-mile trip. The Datsun was our car for that long journey, and it was quite an adventure with the cat meowing the whole way. It was a memorable trip that involved moving and saying goodbye to neighbors. The Datsun was part of our family life at that time.

In the late 1910s, I went to Tulsa after my uncle lost his money and moved there. I attended Tulsa Business College but struggled financially. I worked in various jobs including at my uncle's store and later got a job with the wealthy Travis family. Tulsa was an important place in my early adult life as I tried to find work and continue my education.

Around 1917 or 1918, I attended the National Automobile School to learn mechanical work after my uncle lost his money. I graduated from the school but found the garage work greasy and not suitable for me since I wanted to stay presentable. Although I completed the course, I didn't continue in the mechanical field because it was too dirty and difficult to maintain a proper appearance.

While in Oklahoma, I was told by my uncle to go to Sand Springs Park where they had summer games and shops. I operated a ping pong game there for two seasons, which was a small business venture. Although I made some money, it was not enough to support myself fully. Sand Springs was part of my early efforts to earn a living before moving on to other opportunities.

In Tulsa, I met a Japanese man who worked for the wealthy Travis family, and through him, I got a job with the Travis Corporation. I worked there for about six months earning $75 a month, which was good pay at the time. The job was better than others I had, but it still wasn't enough to get me to dental college. The Travis family and their corporation played a key role in my early work life in Tulsa.

After realizing I couldn't afford dental college in Tulsa, I followed a friend to Chicago because it had many dental schools. I took a job as a butler in a wealthy family, which helped me earn money while attending art school. Life in Chicago was challenging with tight finances, but I managed to go to the Art Institute and work simultaneously. Chicago was a turning point in my pursuit of education and work.

I attended the Art Institute of Chicago, located on Michigan Boulevard, while working as a butler to support myself. The school cost $20 a month, and I earned $7 a week, which made it difficult financially. I walked about 17 blocks to get to the school and enjoyed visiting the museum there. Although I tried to pursue art, I was advised that being an artist was a poor business, which influenced my decisions later.

Michigan Boulevard in Chicago was where the Art Institute was located, and I walked about 17 blocks there every day. It was a long walk but part of my daily routine while I was studying art and working as a butler. The area was also near Central Park, where I went to see art exhibits. Michigan Boulevard was central to my experience in Chicago during that time.

While in Chicago, I visited Central Park near Michigan Boulevard on Sundays to see art exhibits. It was part of my effort to immerse myself in the art world while attending the Art Institute. These visits helped me understand the life of an artist, although I was warned that art was not a reliable way to make money. Central Park was a place of reflection during my time as a student and worker in Chicago.
